title: Fitting a toilet pan connector
subtitle: Connect a replaced toilet pan to an existing soil stack socket
excerpt: How to fit a pan connector between a toilet pan outlet and the soil stack: measuring the offset, choosing the correct connector type, cutting to length, and seating for a watertight connection.

glossaryTerms.definition: A moulded plastic connector that links the toilet pan outlet to the soil stack socket. Available in straight, angled (90-degree), and adjustable push-fit types to accommodate horizontal offsets, height differences, and pipe angles. The pan connector forms the only watertight joint between the toilet and the soil drainage system.

content.text: A
pan connector

bridges the gap between the toilet pan outlet and the soil stack. Most DIY toilet replacements fail at this step: the new pan sits at a slightly different position than the old one, and the connector type chosen does not accommodate the offset. Measure first; buy the connector second.
When to use it
Replacing the toilet pan where the pan outlet and stack socket positions match the old pan.
Relocating a toilet pan slightly from its existing position.
Resealing a leaking pan-to-stack connection where the old connector has cracked or pulled out.
Step-by-step
Step 1, measure the offset.

With the pan in its final position, measure the horizontal distance from the pan outlet centre to the stack socket centre, and the height difference between the two. A straight push-fit connector handles zero offset; a 90-degree connector handles a back-to-wall soil pipe; an adjustable multi-fit connector handles offsets up to 50 mm in any direction.
Step 2, choose and cut the connector.

Most adjustable connectors are sold overlong and cut to length. Fit the connector loosely on the pan outlet, mark the cut line at the correct length for the stack socket, and cut with the utility knife on a flat surface. Deburr the cut end with the knife blade or a piece of emery cloth.
Step 3, fit the connector.

Push the pan end of the connector fully onto the pan outlet. The pan outlet taper should bottom out inside the connector rubber seal. Push the connector end fully into the stack socket; a firm push by hand seats it. The rubber socket seal holds the connection; no adhesive is required.
Step 4, position and fix the pan.

Slide the pan back over the connector into its final position. The connector should not be under tension in any direction. Fix the pan to the floor with the screws provided, but do not overtighten: ceramic pans crack under torque. Check the connector is not bowed or kinked; a bowed connector will leak at the stack socket.
Common mistakes
Buying a straight connector when an angled one is needed.
Measure the offset before buying. A straight connector forced into an angled installation will pull out of the socket under load.
Leaving the connector under lateral tension.
If the pan has to be pushed hard to reach the stack socket, the connector is too short or the pan is in the wrong position. Adjust before fixing the pan to the floor.
